response:
All three advisors converge on **Option C (O3, intermediate cap with planned revisit)** at score 5, while O1 (1,000/day) and O2 (150/day) both scored lower (2 and 4) — and the two rounds show no real disagreement to resolve. I'm going with O3.

## Decision Record: Free-tier daily request cap

**Decision:** Cap the free tier at **300 requests/day per key**, effective at launch, with a revisit checkpoint tied to real Pro-conversion data.

**Why 300, specifically:**
- It sits above every observed day in the 30-day log (busiest ~250/key-avg), so no current free user gets capped or breaks on day one — no launch-week support fires.
- It's well below the built/load-tested 1,000, so it isn't a wall-free-forever number. As usage grows past today's baseline, active users will hit it and become real upgrade candidates.
- It's deliberately not "near observed usage" (150) — the memo is explicit that conversion elasticity is an unvalidated guess, and a harder cap on zero data risks pushing users to churn instead of upgrade. 300 gets most of the lever effect while hedging that risk.

**Revisit mechanism (the part that keeps this from being O1/O2 with extra steps):**
- Instrument a "hit the cap" event per key now, at launch — not something to add later.
- Hard checkpoint: **90 days after Pro goes live**, review (a) what fraction of capped keys converted to Pro vs. churned, and (b) what fraction of keys are hitting 300 at all.
- This is a calendar commitment, not a "someday" — put 2026-10-12 (90 days post-launch, adjust once Pro ships) on the calendar now.

**What would make me change the number:**
- If capped users churn rather than upgrade → raise the cap toward 1,000; the lever is costing more users than it converts.
- If almost nobody reaches 300 even months after Pro launches → lower it toward 150; it isn't functioning as a lever at all.
- If overage triggers meaningful support load before the 90-day mark → tighten enforcement (e.g., soft-warn then hard-cap) rather than changing the number itself.

No file changes needed — this is a standalone decision, not something the repo's code depends on. Let me know if you want this appended to `MEMO.md` as a resolution section.

options.label: Cap at 1,000/day
options.summary: Set the free-tier cap at 1,000 requests/day, matching the already-built and load-tested metering capacity.

options.label: Cap near observed usage (~150/day)
options.summary: Set the cap close to typical active-key usage (~100-250/day) so it functions as a conversion lever toward Pro.

options.label: Intermediate cap with planned revisit
options.summary: Choose a cap between observed usage and built capacity, with a defined point to reassess once Pro conversion data exists.

O1.crux: A 1,000/day cap sits ~4-10x above even the busiest observed per-key day (~250), so it costs nothing to build but functions as no wall at all — free stays free forever
O1.dependency: If the goal is purely to avoid support/infra risk rather than to drive Pro conversion, then matching built capacity is the right call and this score should rise

O2.crux: A ~150/day cap sits just above typical usage (~100/key) but below the busiest days (~250), making it a real lever that active users will plausibly hit within weeks without punishing typical usage on day one
O2.dependency: If actual conversion sensitivity to hitting a cap turns out to be low (users just churn instead of upgrading, since there's no Pro conversion data yet), the lever could suppress usage/goodwill without producing revenue, reversing this score

O3.crux: Given the memo's own admission that conversion elasticity is an unvalidated guess, an intermediate cap with a defined revisit point captures most of the conversion-lever effect while hedging against picking the wrong number on zero data
O3.dependency: If there's no realistic mechanism/discipline to actually revisit the cap later (i.e., 'planned revisit' never happens), this collapses into an arbitrary middle number with none of the hedge's benefit, reversing this score

O1.crux: 1,000/day is ~10x the observed per-key average (~94-106/day across 30 days), so it forfeits the cap's stated purpose as a conversion lever purely because the capacity happens to already exist.
O1.dependency: If the free tier's real job is cost/abuse containment rather than driving Pro conversion (e.g. revenue isn't the near-term goal), then 'already load-tested for 1,000' stops being irrelevant sunk cost and becomes the correct low-risk choice, reversing the score upward.

O2.crux: 150/day sits just above the observed daily average (~100, max ~106) and directly operationalizes the memo's own framing of the cap as a conversion lever.
O2.dependency: The CSV only gives daily aggregates (total_requests/active_keys), not per-key distributions — if a meaningful subset of legitimate free users runs far above the ~100 average on any given day (long-tail usage), a 150 cap would throttle real engaged users rather than cleanly separating light users from upgrade candidates, reversing the score downward.

O3.crux: The memo states outright that conversion elasticity is 'the guess the entire decision rides on' with zero Pro data yet — an intermediate cap with a defined revisit point is the calibrated response to that admitted uncertainty rather than committing hard in either direction.
O3.dependency: If there's no real mechanism or discipline (as a solo founder) to actually execute the revisit later, the 'planned revisit' is a promise with no teeth, and O3 degrades into an arbitrary number with the appearance of rigor but none of the benefit over just picking O1 or O2 outright.

O1.crux: Matches built capacity, but sizing the rig for 1,000 was arbitrary from day one and has no bearing on what cap actually converts users, so 'costs nothing to build' isn't a reason to pick it as the business number.
O1.dependency: If the founder's real goal were purely technical headroom/safety margin rather than a conversion lever, this would be defensible — but the memo explicitly frames the cap as a conversion lever, which this option ignores.

O2.crux: Setting the cap near the ~100-250/day observed range makes it a real, binding conversion lever now instead of waiting on data the founder admits doesn't exist yet.
O2.dependency: If the guess that a binding cap meaningfully drives free-to-Pro conversion is wrong (e.g., users churn instead of upgrading when capped), this aggressive a cut could cost more active users than it converts.

O3.crux: It captures the same conversion-lever benefit as O2 while hedging against the admitted uncertainty in conversion behavior, with a defined revisit point once real Pro data exists.
O3.dependency: If there's no credible commitment/mechanism to actually revisit the number later (i.e., 'planned revisit' is vague and never happens), it collapses into a slightly-better-hedged version of O1 with false confidence.

O1.dependency: If the goal is purely to avoid support/infra risk rather than to drive Pro conversion, then matching built capacity is the right call and this score should rise
